title: Using a chicken tractor on pasture
subtitle: Moving a portable run to manage grass and reduce parasite build-up.
excerpt: A chicken tractor is a floorless coop and run on skids or wheels that moves daily or weekly across a paddock. Chickens get access to fresh grass and insects; the ground gets a rest before it's colonised by parasites. The technique works on a patch as small as fifty square metres.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA code of practice for the welfare of meat chickens and laying hens, gov.uk. FAO Small-scale poultry production, FAO Animal Production and Health Manual (2004). University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension, Pastured Poultry Systems.

glossaryTerms.definition: A portable floorless poultry run, usually with a small shelter attached, moved regularly across pasture so birds have access to fresh ground.
Moving livestock between sections of ground on a cycle that allows each section to rest and recover before animals return.

content.text: manages two problems that a fixed coop creates: poached ground and worm-burden accumulation. Chickens in a fixed run will strip the vegetation and turn the soil to compacted mud within a few weeks in all but the driest summers. Coccidiosis and roundworm build up in soiled ground and are re-ingested on every scratch.

Move the tractor before the grass is stripped to bare soil. In summer on good growing ground, three to four days per position. In autumn or on poorer turf, every one to two days. The guide is the ground condition, not the calendar: when scratching is down to soil and there's visible trampling, move.

A four-metre by two-metre tractor with five hens needs to move every two to three days in summer. Plan a circuit around your available paddock so each position gets at least three weeks' rest before the birds return. Three weeks is the minimum for grass to recover and for the coccidia oocyst population to fall significantly.

The tractor needs to be light enough to move by one person. Timber-framed tractors for five birds should weigh under 40 kg. Wheels or skids fitted to one end allow you to tilt and drag without lifting the whole structure. Build the frame from 25 mm timber; larger sections make the structure heavy without adding useful strength.

The floor is open to the ground. The sides must be predator-proof: 13 mm welded wire mesh (not hexagonal chicken wire) on every face, including the roof. Ground predators can lift ordinary chicken wire if there's no floor to block them; welded mesh is harder to deform.

Foxes are active at dusk and dawn; a tractor without a secure coop section is adequate for daytime ranging but not for overnight. The tractor needs either a fully enclosed shelter the birds lock into at night, or a separate night-time coop you move them to and from. A pop-hole with a drop latch is not the same as a secure box.

Hang the feeder and drinker from the tractor roof so they move with the unit and don't contact the ground. Ground-level feeders on pasture fill with soil and attract rodents. Birds on good pasture will reduce their feed intake; check consumption rather than topping up on a fixed schedule.

doesn't need to be complex. Mark your circuit with pegs. Work down one side of the paddock in daily moves, then work back on the opposite track two weeks later. The simplest circuits use four to six positions and return the tractor to the first position when the grass has recovered.

Overwintering on pasture is harder. Ground that doesn't drain well will poach regardless of rotation speed. In wet winters, consider moving the birds into a more sheltered system from November to March and returning to tractor rotation when the ground firms.
